backend_questions.question: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous operations in Node.js?
backend_questions.answer: Synchronous operations block execution until completion. Asynchronous operations don't block; they complete in background and notify via callbacks, promises, or events. Node.js uses non-blocking I/O by default, enabling handling multiple concurrent requests without creating threads.

backend_questions.question: Explain the event-driven architecture of Node.js.
backend_questions.answer: Node.js uses event-driven, non-blocking I/O model. Components: Event Emitters generate events, Event Listeners respond to events. EventEmitter class allows custom events. Pattern enables handling multiple concurrent requests efficiently with single thread.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of middleware in Express.js?
backend_questions.answer: Middleware functions have access to request and response objects in request-response cycle. Can execute code, modify request/response, end request-response, or call next middleware. Used for logging, authentication, parsing request bodies, error handling, CORS.

backend_questions.question: What is the difference between REST and GraphQL APIs?
backend_questions.answer: REST uses fixed endpoints with HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE). GraphQL uses single endpoint with query language, allowing clients to request exactly needed data. REST requires multiple requests for related data; GraphQL fetches complex structures in one request. REST simpler; GraphQL more flexible.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of routing in Express.js.
backend_questions.answer: Routing defines how application responds to client requests. Express routes: app.get(), app.post(), app.put(), app.delete(). Routes map URL patterns to handler functions. Support wildcards, regex patterns, route parameters. Enable RESTful API design.

backend_questions.question: What is the difference between SQL and NoSQL databases?
backend_questions.answer: SQL databases use structured schemas, tables, ACID transactions, complex queries. NoSQL databases are schema-less, document/key-value based, eventual consistency, horizontal scaling. SQL for relational data and complex queries; NoSQL for unstructured data and scalability.

backend_questions.question: What is authentication and authorization?
backend_questions.answer: Authentication verifies user identity (login credentials). Authorization determines what authenticated user can access (permissions). Must authenticate before authorization. Authentication answers 'Who are you?', authorization answers 'What can you do?'.

backend_questions.question: Explain JWT (JSON Web Token) and how it works.
backend_questions.answer: JWT is compact token for stateless authentication. Consists of header (type/algorithm), payload (claims/data), signature. Server signs token, client sends in requests. Server verifies signature without session storage. Enables distributed authentication across services.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of environment variables?
backend_questions.answer: Environment variables store configuration outside code. Keep sensitive data secure (API keys, passwords, database URLs). Different values per environment (dev, test, prod). In Node.js, access via process.env. Use .env files with dotenv package.

backend_questions.question: What is CORS and why is it important?
backend_questions.answer: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) controls cross-origin requests. Browsers enforce same-origin policy for security. Server must set CORS headers (Access-Control-Allow-Origin) for cross-origin requests. Prevents unauthorized cross-domain requests. Essential for modern web applications.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of ORM (Object-Relational Mapping).
backend_questions.answer: ORM converts between incompatible type systems (objects and relational databases). Maps database tables to classes/objects. Allows object-oriented code instead of raw SQL. Examples: Sequelize, TypeORM, Prisma. Simplifies database operations.

backend_questions.question: What is connection pooling and why is it important?
backend_questions.answer: Connection pooling maintains cache of reusable database connections. Instead of creating new connections per request (expensive), reuses existing ones. Improves performance, reduces resource consumption. Critical for high-traffic applications. Configurable pool size.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of database migrations?
backend_questions.answer: Migrations version control database schema changes. Track table creation, column additions, modifications, indexes. Enable consistent schema across environments. Tools: Knex, Sequelize, Alembic. Reversible (rollback capability). Essential for team development.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of database indexing.
backend_questions.answer: Index is data structure improving query performance. Creates sorted map of column values. Without indexes, queries scan all rows (slow). With indexes, queries find data faster (especially WHERE clauses, JOINs). Trade-off: slower writes, more storage. Use wisely.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of database transactions?
backend_questions.answer: Transaction is sequence of operations treated as single unit. Either all succeed (commit) or all fail (rollback). ACID properties: Atomicity (all/nothing), Consistency (valid state), Isolation (concurrent transactions don't interfere), Durability (persists after commit).

backend_questions.question: What is the difference between HTTP POST and PUT?
backend_questions.answer: POST creates new resource (not idempotent, location varies). PUT replaces entire resource (idempotent, same location). POST typically returns 201 (Created), PUT returns 200 (OK). POST for creating, PUT for replacing. Some use PATCH for partial updates.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of REST API design principles.
backend_questions.answer: REST uses HTTP methods for operations: GET (retrieve), POST (create), PUT (replace), DELETE (remove), PATCH (partial update). Resources identified by URLs. Stateless (no server-side sessions). Cacheable responses. Client-server separation. Enables scalable, decoupled systems.

backend_questions.question: What are HTTP status codes and their meanings?
backend_questions.answer: 2xx: Success (200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content). 3xx: Redirection (301 Moved Permanently, 304 Not Modified). 4xx: Client errors (400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found). 5xx: Server errors (500 Internal Server, 503 Unavailable).

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of API versioning?
backend_questions.answer: API versioning allows backward compatibility while evolving API. Strategies: URL versioning (/v1/users), header versioning, query parameters. Prevents breaking client applications. Enables gradual migration to new versions. Plan versioning strategy upfront.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of request validation.
backend_questions.answer: Request validation ensures data meets requirements before processing. Validates type, format, length, range. Prevents invalid data in database. Libraries: Joi, Yup, express-validator. Reduces errors, improves security. Return 400 Bad Request for invalid data.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of error handling middleware?
backend_questions.answer: Error middleware catches and handles errors in Express. Defined with 4 parameters: (err, req, res, next). Logs errors, sends appropriate responses, prevents crashes. Place after other middleware. Send error status codes (4xx or 5xx). Enables graceful error recovery.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of logging in backend applications.
backend_questions.answer: Logging records events, errors, and activities. Enables debugging, performance monitoring, audit trails. Libraries: Winston, Morgan, Bunyan. Log levels: debug, info, warn, error. Write to files or services (ELK, Splunk). Essential for production systems.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of caching in backend systems?
backend_questions.answer: Caching stores frequently accessed data for quick retrieval. Reduces database queries, API calls, computation. Types: in-memory (Redis), database query caching, HTTP caching, object caching. Trade-off: stale data if not invalidated. Critical for performance.

backend_questions.question: What is the difference between Redis and Memcached?
backend_questions.answer: Redis is in-memory data store with persistence and complex data types. Memcached simple key-value caching. Redis supports strings, lists, sets, hashes, sorted sets. Memcached only strings. Redis has durability options; Memcached not. Redis widely used for caching, sessions, pub-sub.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of session management.
backend_questions.answer: Session management maintains user state across requests. Server creates session with unique ID, stores in cookies. Allows tracking authentication, preferences, shopping carts. Sessions expire after inactivity. Can use database, Redis, or in-memory store. Security: use secure, httpOnly cookies.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of rate limiting?
backend_questions.answer: Rate limiting restricts API requests to prevent abuse. Strategies: token bucket, sliding window, leaky bucket. Return 429 (Too Many Requests) when exceeded. Protect against DDoS, prevent server overload. Libraries: express-rate-limit. Include rate limit headers in response.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of API throttling.
backend_questions.answer: Throttling limits request rate per user or IP. Ensures fair resource distribution. Different from rate limiting (hard limit). Allows requests but slows down rate. Queue or delay excess requests. Important for public APIs and shared resources.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of dependency injection?
backend_questions.answer: Dependency injection provides dependencies externally rather than creating internally. Improves testability, flexibility, reduces tight coupling. In Node.js: pass dependencies as parameters or use DI containers. Enables mocking in tests.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of SOLID principles in backend design.
backend_questions.answer: Single Responsibility (one reason to change), Open/Closed (open for extension, closed for modification), Liskov Substitution (derived classes replaceable), Interface Segregation (client-specific interfaces), Dependency Inversion (depend on abstractions). Enable maintainable, scalable code.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of API documentation?
backend_questions.answer: API documentation describes endpoints, parameters, responses. Tools: Swagger/OpenAPI, Postman, API Blueprint. Auto-generates interactive explorers. Improves developer experience, enables client code generation. Essential for APIs used by others. Keep updated with code.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of WebSockets in backend.
backend_questions.answer: WebSockets establish persistent, full-duplex communication between client and server. Unlike HTTP (request-response), WebSockets allow bidirectional real-time data exchange. Used for chat, notifications, live updates. Libraries: Socket.io, ws. Requires upgrade from HTTP handshake.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of message queues?
backend_questions.answer: Message queues decouple services, enabling asynchronous communication. Producer sends messages, consumer processes them. Improves reliability, scalability, resilience. Services work independently. Examples: RabbitMQ, Kafka, AWS SQS. Ensures messages processed even if consumer offline.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of pub-sub (publish-subscribe).
backend_questions.answer: Pub-sub is messaging pattern: publishers send messages to topics, subscribers listen to topics. Decouples publishers and subscribers. Many subscribers per topic. Real-time event distribution. Examples: Redis Pub-Sub, Kafka, RabbitMQ. Enables reactive architectures.

backend_questions.question: What is the difference between monolithic and microservices architecture?
backend_questions.answer: Monolithic: single large application with all functionality. Microservices: small, independent services. Monolithic simpler initially, becomes complex at scale. Microservices complex deployment, enable independent scaling. Monolithic tightly coupled; microservices loosely coupled. Choose based on team, scale.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of containerization with Docker.
backend_questions.answer: Docker containerizes applications into isolated environments with all dependencies. Ensures consistency across dev, test, production. Lightweight, portable, scale easily. Dockerfile defines image, containers run from images. Improves DevOps, simplifies deployment.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of load balancing?
backend_questions.answer: Load balancing distributes traffic across multiple servers. Improves availability, prevents overload, enables scaling. Algorithms: round-robin, least connections, IP hash, weighted. Can be hardware or software (Nginx, HAProxy). Critical for high-traffic applications.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of reverse proxy.
backend_questions.answer: Reverse proxy sits between clients and servers. Forwards client requests to backend servers. Handles load balancing, caching, SSL termination, compression. Hides server details. Improves security, performance, scalability. Examples: Nginx, Apache.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of API rate limiting headers?
backend_questions.answer: Rate limit headers inform clients of their rate limit status. Standard headers: X-RateLimit-Limit (max requests), X-RateLimit-Remaining (requests left), X-RateLimit-Reset (reset time). Clients adjust behavior based on headers. Improves API usage management.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of database sharding.
backend_questions.answer: Sharding horizontally partitions data across multiple servers. Data distributed by shard key. Each shard stores subset of data. Improves scalability, performance. Complex queries may require querying multiple shards. Requires application awareness of sharding.

backend_questions.question: What is the difference between normalization and denormalization?
backend_questions.answer: Normalization reduces data redundancy, improves data integrity. Splits data into multiple tables. Requires JOINs for queries. Slower reads, faster writes. Denormalization duplicates data for faster queries. Slower writes, faster reads. Trade-off based on access patterns.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of ACID properties in databases.
backend_questions.answer: Atomicity: all or nothing (all operations succeed or all fail). Consistency: database moves from valid state to valid state. Isolation: concurrent transactions don't interfere. Durability: committed data persists. Fundamental for data reliability. NoSQL often sacrifices for performance.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of query optimization?
backend_questions.answer: Query optimization improves database performance. Techniques: use indexes, limit result sets, avoid N+1 queries, use proper JOINs, analyze query plans. Tools: EXPLAIN, query analyzers. Monitor slow queries. Critical for scaling databases.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of N+1 query problem.
backend_questions.answer: N+1 problem occurs when fetching related data requires N+1 queries (1 parent, N children). Example: get users then loop fetching posts (1 + N queries). Solutions: eager loading, batch loading, joins, select specific columns. Identify with query monitoring.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of database views?
backend_questions.answer: Views are virtual tables based on queries. Simplify complex queries. Provide data abstraction, security (limit exposed columns). Updatable views modify underlying tables. Read-only views aggregate data. Improve code readability, reduce repetition.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of stored procedures.
backend_questions.answer: Stored procedures are precompiled SQL code stored in database. Execute with CALL statement. Benefits: reusability, security, performance, reduced network traffic. Downsides: harder to version control, test, debug. Less popular with modern ORMs.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of API pagination?
backend_questions.answer: Pagination divides large result sets into smaller pages. Parameters: page number, page size, offset. Improves performance, reduces memory usage. Standards: limit/offset, cursor-based, keyset. Return total count for UI. Cursor-based better for large datasets.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of API filtering and sorting.
backend_questions.answer: Filtering narrows results by criteria (e.g., ?status=active). Sorting orders results by field (e.g., ?sort=name). Implement with query parameters. Enable flexible data retrieval. Validate filter/sort fields for security. Combine with pagination for performance.

backend_questions.question: What is the difference between stateless and stateful services?
backend_questions.answer: Stateless services don't store session data; each request independent. Easier to scale horizontally, no session affinity needed. Stateful services store session data locally. Harder to scale, require session affinity or shared storage. Modern services prefer stateless design.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of idempotency in APIs.
backend_questions.answer: Idempotency means same request produces same result repeatedly. GET, PUT, DELETE are idempotent. POST not idempotent. Implement with idempotency keys for POST. Prevents duplicate operations on retries. Critical for reliability.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of API request/response compression?
backend_questions.answer: Compression reduces data size sent over network. Gzip, Brotli algorithms. Accept-Encoding header specifies supported compression. Improves bandwidth, latency. Server compression adds CPU cost. Enable for text-based responses (JSON, HTML). Transparent to clients.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of write-ahead logging in databases.
backend_questions.answer: Write-ahead logging (WAL) writes transactions to log before applying to database. Ensures durability: if crash, log allows recovery. Improves performance by batching writes. Fundamental for ACID compliance, database reliability.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of database backups?
backend_questions.answer: Backups protect against data loss from failures, corruption, disasters. Types: full (entire database), incremental (changes since last backup), differential. Frequency: daily, hourly, or continuous. Test restore procedures. Critical for production systems.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of database replication.
backend_questions.answer: Replication copies data from primary database to replicas. Improves availability, enables failover. Replication lag: replicas slightly behind primary. Master-slave: write to master, read from slaves. Master-master: write to any. Increases durability.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of API versioning strategies?
backend_questions.answer: Versioning strategies: URL (/v1/users), header (Accept: application/vnd.api+json;version=1), query (?version=1), media type. URL most common, clear in documentation. Header flexible, hides versioning. Query parameter less common. Choose consistent strategy.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of graceful shutdown.
backend_questions.answer: Graceful shutdown stops accepting new requests while finishing existing ones. Stop listening to new connections, wait for in-flight requests. Set timeout for forceful shutdown. Enable zero-downtime deployments. Implement with signal handlers (SIGTERM, SIGINT).

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of health checks in services?
backend_questions.answer: Health checks verify service is functioning. Endpoints: /health, /ping. Check dependencies (database, cache, external APIs). Return status codes (200 healthy, 503 unhealthy). Load balancers use health checks for routing. Monitor health metrics.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of circuit breakers.
backend_questions.answer: Circuit breakers prevent cascading failures. States: closed (normal), open (fail fast, don't call), half-open (test recovery). Monitor failure rate, timeout. Recover by testing (half-open). Resilience pattern for distributed systems. Libraries: Polly, resilience4j.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of API request timeouts?
backend_questions.answer: Timeouts prevent hanging requests. Connection timeout: wait establishing connection. Read timeout: wait for response data. Set reasonable values. Prevent resource exhaustion, improve user experience. Default timeouts vary by client/framework.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of retry logic in APIs.
backend_questions.answer: Retry logic retries failed requests. Exponential backoff: increase wait time (2, 4, 8 seconds). Max retries limit. Idempotent operations safe to retry. Non-idempotent operations risky. Only retry on transient errors (timeout, connection refused).

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of API batching?
backend_questions.answer: Batching combines multiple requests into single request. Reduces network overhead, improves performance. Client collects requests, sends in batch. Server processes batch, returns results. Useful for high-volume operations. More complex implementation.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of partial response in APIs.
backend_questions.answer: Partial response returns only requested fields. Parameter: ?fields=id,name,email. Reduces payload size, improves performance. Client specifies needed data. Flexible over fixed responses. Implement field filtering in response serialization.

backend_questions.question: What is the difference between request and response caching?
backend_questions.answer: Request caching skips unnecessary calls (client-side). Response caching stores computed results. HTTP caching controlled by Cache-Control headers. ETags enable conditional requests. Vary header caches variants. Cache validation: fresh vs stale. Critical for performance.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of content negotiation.
backend_questions.answer: Content negotiation allows server and client to agree on format. Accept header specifies client preferences. Server returns requested format (JSON, XML, CSV). Return 406 Not Acceptable if format unsupported. Enables flexible APIs.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of API security headers?
backend_questions.answer: Security headers protect against attacks. X-Frame-Options (clickjacking), X-Content-Type-Options (MIME sniffing), X-XSS-Protection (XSS), Strict-Transport-Security (HTTPS), Content-Security-Policy (XSS/injection). Set in response headers. Implement comprehensive security.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of SQL injection prevention.
backend_questions.answer: SQL injection exploits improperly escaped SQL. Prevention: parameterized queries (prepared statements), input validation, ORMs, stored procedures. Never concatenate user input into SQL. Use placeholders for values. Critical security measure.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of password hashing?
backend_questions.answer: Password hashing converts passwords to fixed-length strings. One-way function (can't reverse). Salting adds random data preventing rainbow table attacks. Algorithms: bcrypt, Argon2, scrypt. Never store plaintext passwords. Use strong hashing for security.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of OAuth and OpenID Connect.
backend_questions.answer: OAuth is authorization framework for delegated access. User authorizes app to access resources. OpenID Connect adds authentication layer (identity). Examples: Google login, GitHub OAuth. Enables single sign-on, third-party authentication. More secure than storing passwords.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of API key management?
backend_questions.answer: API keys authenticate applications. Store securely (environment variables, vault). Rotate regularly. Revoke compromised keys. Rate limit per key. Track usage. Prevent hardcoding in code. Use secrets management tools (HashiCorp Vault).

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of two-factor authentication (2FA).
backend_questions.answer: 2FA requires two verification methods. Factors: something you know (password), have (phone/key), are (fingerprint). Improves security significantly. Methods: SMS, authenticator apps, biometric. Implement optional or mandatory based on sensitivity.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of request signing?
backend_questions.answer: Request signing proves request authenticity and integrity. Sign request with private key, client verifies with public key. Prevents tampering. Used in: AWS Signature Version 4, JWT. Ensures request not modified in transit.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of input sanitization.
backend_questions.answer: Sanitization removes harmful characters from input. Prevents injection attacks (SQL, XSS). Libraries: DOMPurify, sanitize-html. Validate input format, type, length. Whitelist allowed characters. Critical security measure.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of HTTPS/SSL-TLS?
backend_questions.answer: HTTPS encrypts data in transit using SSL/TLS. Prevents interception, man-in-the-middle attacks. Requires SSL certificate. Handshake establishes encryption. Modern standard (HTTP/2, HTTP/3 require HTTPS). Essential for security.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of API versioning deprecation.
backend_questions.answer: Deprecation plan removes old API versions. Announce deprecation date, timeline. Support old version temporarily. Redirect to new version. Log deprecation warnings. Communicate with API consumers. Manage technically debt.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of monitoring and observability?
backend_questions.answer: Monitoring tracks metrics (CPU, memory, requests). Observability understands system behavior (logs, traces, metrics). Enable debugging issues, performance optimization. Tools: Prometheus, Datadog, New Relic, ELK stack. Essential for production systems.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of distributed tracing.
backend_questions.answer: Distributed tracing tracks requests across services. Assigns unique trace IDs, propagates through services. Shows request path, timing, errors. Tools: Jaeger, Zipkin, AWS X-Ray. Enables debugging microservices interactions.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of application performance monitoring (APM)?
backend_questions.answer: APM monitors application performance. Tracks response times, throughput, error rates. Identifies bottlenecks, slow endpoints. Correlates with infrastructure metrics. Tools: New Relic, Datadog, AppDynamics. Enables performance optimization.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of Service Level Agreements (SLAs).
backend_questions.answer: SLA defines service reliability commitments. Metrics: uptime percentage (99.9%), response time, error rates. Penalties if SLA violated. Drive architecture, monitoring decisions. Examples: 99.9% uptime = max 43 minutes downtime per month.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of chaos engineering?
backend_questions.answer: Chaos engineering intentionally injects failures to test resilience. Identifies weak points, validates recovery procedures. Tools: Chaos Toolkit, Gremlin. Run in controlled environment. Improves reliability before production incidents.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of database query caching.
backend_questions.answer: Query result caching stores frequently queried data. Reduces database load, improves response time. Invalidate on data changes. Tools: Redis, Memcached. Trade-off: stale data vs performance. Implement careful invalidation strategy.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of background jobs?
backend_questions.answer: Background jobs execute tasks asynchronously. Examples: sending emails, processing images, generating reports. Improves responsiveness (don't block requests). Tools: Bull, RabbitMQ, Celery. Enable reliable, scalable background processing.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of webhook in backend.
backend_questions.answer: Webhook is HTTP callback for event notifications. App sends POST to webhook URL when event occurs. Client subscribes to events, receives real-time notifications. Alternative to polling. Examples: payment notifications, GitHub events.

backend_questions.question: What is the difference between webhooks and polling?
backend_questions.answer: Webhooks: server pushes data to client (real-time). Polling: client periodically requests data. Webhooks more efficient, lower latency. Polling simpler, doesn't require exposed endpoints. Webhooks better for frequent updates.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of API documentation with Swagger/OpenAPI.
backend_questions.answer: Swagger/OpenAPI standardizes API documentation. YAML/JSON specification describes endpoints, parameters, responses. Auto-generates interactive Swagger UI. Enables client code generation, testing. Improves API usability, developer experience.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of API testing frameworks?
backend_questions.answer: API testing validates APIs work correctly. Frameworks: Jest, Supertest, Mocha. Tests: status codes, response structure, data, error handling. Enable test automation, CI/CD integration. Ensures API reliability.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of test-driven development (TDD).
backend_questions.answer: TDD writes tests before implementation. Red-Green-Refactor cycle: write failing test (red), implement minimal code (green), refactor. Improves code quality, design, reduces bugs. Used in backend development for reliability.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of continuous integration (CI)?
backend_questions.answer: CI automatically builds and tests code on every commit. Catches errors early. Runs unit tests, integration tests, linting. Tools: GitHub Actions, Jenkins, GitLab CI. Enables rapid feedback, prevents regressions.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of continuous deployment (CD).
backend_questions.answer: CD automatically deploys tested code to production. Fully automated pipeline from code to production. Every commit deployable. Enables rapid releases, quick feedback. Tools: Docker, Kubernetes, Jenkins. Requires high test coverage.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of infrastructure as code (IaC)?
backend_questions.answer: IaC manages infrastructure through code. Version control, reproducibility, automation. Tools: Terraform, CloudFormation, Ansible. Define servers, networks, databases in code. Enable consistent, scalable deployments.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of containerization with Kubernetes.
backend_questions.answer: Kubernetes orchestrates containers at scale. Automates deployment, scaling, management. Features: load balancing, self-healing, rolling updates. Container abstraction. Simplifies microservices management. Industry standard.

backend_questions.question: What is the difference between scaling vertically and horizontally?
backend_questions.answer: Vertical scaling: add resources to single server (CPU, memory). Horizontal scaling: add more servers. Vertical limited by hardware. Horizontal improves availability, resilience. Horizontal preferred for modern systems.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of auto-scaling.
backend_questions.answer: Auto-scaling automatically adjusts server count based on demand. Metrics: CPU, memory, request count. Scale up during load, scale down during low usage. Cost efficient. Cloud platforms: AWS Auto Scaling, GCP Autoscaler. Enable elastic systems.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of database indexing strategies?
backend_questions.answer: Indexing strategies optimize query performance. Index frequently searched columns. Composite indexes for multiple columns. Avoid over-indexing (slows writes). Monitor index usage. Tool: EXPLAIN. Balance read/write performance.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of eventual consistency.
backend_questions.answer: Eventual consistency guarantees data becomes consistent after updates. Common in distributed systems, NoSQL databases. Trades consistency for availability, partition tolerance (CAP theorem). Eventual means all replicas eventually converge.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of API rate limiting strategies?
backend_questions.answer: Rate limiting strategies: token bucket (smooth bursts), leaky bucket (smooth output), sliding window (precise), fixed window. Choose based on use case. Different limits for different endpoints/users. Implement graceful degradation.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of API deprecation communication.
backend_questions.answer: Communicate deprecation in multiple ways: API documentation, headers (Deprecation, Sunset), email to users, changelog. Announce timeline (months/years). Support old version during transition. Minimize disruption to API consumers.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of feature flags in backend?
backend_questions.answer: Feature flags toggle features on/off without code deployment. Enable A/B testing, gradual rollouts, quick rollbacks. Control features per user, environment, percentage. Reduces risk of new features. Essential for continuous deployment.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of database warm-up.
backend_questions.answer: Database warm-up pre-loads frequently accessed data. Reduces initial query latency. Done during startup or low-traffic periods. Caches data in memory. Improves user experience after restarts.

backend_questions.question: What is the purpose of service mesh in microservices?
backend_questions.answer: Service mesh manages microservices communication. Handles load balancing, retries, circuit breaking, security. Sidecar proxies (Envoy) per service. Tools: Istio, Linkerd. Simplifies microservices operations, improves reliability.

backend_questions.question: Explain the concept of API gateway.
backend_questions.answer: API gateway is single entry point for APIs. Routes requests to services, handles authentication, rate limiting, logging. Aggregates APIs, transforms requests/responses. Tools: Kong, AWS API Gateway. Simplifies client integration.

backend_questions.question: What is the difference between push and pull in message systems?
backend_questions.answer: Push: message broker sends to consumer (real-time). Pull: consumer requests messages (batch processing). Push ensures immediate delivery but requires consumer availability. Pull allows batching, better for high-volume. Choose based on latency requirements.
